## Chronology Spine

## Core Truth Set
- (5/5) Fire practice is framed as maintenance discipline, not fuel-maximizing intensity, with micro-adjustments rather than forceful intervention. | Quote: "The fire was not something to feed. It was something to maintain."
- (5/5) The house is portrayed as a thermal-memory system that holds and redistributes heat, scent, and labor traces across time. | Quote: "The house held heat differently ... it settled into the walls, into the ground, into the objects that lived there."
- (5/5) Domestic order is described as intentional placement shaped by repeated use rather than decorative arrangement. | Quote: "Nothing was arranged. Everything was placed."
- (4/5) Training occurs through sensory literacy (texture, heat, smell, rhythm) and embodied repetition rather than abstract explanation. | Quote: "Ground corn and water ... thick ... beginning to shift ... the clay was warm to the touch."

## Setting Anchors
- Hearth-Centered Household Workspace [HOME]: Curved-pole, cane-clay domestic interior organized around a low central hearth and continuous productive rhythm.

## Continuity Constraints
- Maintenance-Not-Force House Law: Domestic governance constraint.
- Correction as Placement: Training interaction constraint.
- No-Wasted-Motion Discipline: Embodied-discipline constraint.

## Open Research Gaps
- What thresholds trigger correction versus silent observation in novice household training? (priority 2): Needed to calibrate authority tone and learning progression.
- What labor rotation rules determine who tends fire, grain, sorting, and repair work across age and status? (priority 2): Needed for continuity in domestic scenes and role assignment realism.
- How often are cane/clay wall repairs and hearth adjustments made across seasonal cycles? (priority 2): Needed to ground environmental wear-and-maintenance detail in recurring practice.

## Next Chapter Assembly Rule
- Do not promote a chapter beat to the core story unless it references at least one core claim, one chronology anchor (or explicit open gap), and one continuity constraint.
